# KarirKalyan

> Full-stack job application tracker — Rails 8 API + Next.js 16 frontend

KarirKalyan is a web application for tracking job applications through a finite state machine (FSM)
pipeline. Built as a portfolio project by Chairul Akmal (https://chairulakmal.com).

## What it does

- Tracks job applications across 13 FSM states. The pipeline runs wishlist → draft → applied →
  phone_screen → technical → final_round → offer, ending in accepted, declined, rejected,
  ghosted, withdrawn or archived. It is not linear: ghosted, rejected and withdrawn can all
  return to applied. The transition table in api/app/lib/application_fsm.rb is the only
  authority on which moves are legal — this list is a summary and may lag it.
- Writes an immutable TimelineEntry on every status transition (full audit trail)
- Sends follow-up reminders via idempotent Solid Queue background jobs, batched into one digest
  per user per day rather than one email per application. The digest holds on weekends, Japanese
  national holidays, New Year, Golden Week and Obon, and the held reminders go out on the next
  business day — deferred, not dropped.
- Exports your data two ways: a CSV of your applications, and a full-account zip containing
  account.json plus every resume and cover letter you uploaded. Both are downloads from the
  dashboard; the zip is the one that can actually restore an account.
- Stores resume and cover letter as PostgreSQL bytea per application: 1 MB each, and at most 200
  applications per account, which is what bounds total storage — a rate limit cannot, since every
  window resets. Downloaded PDFs are named after the application they belong to
  (company-role-MMDD-id-kind.pdf), by one method shared with the archive, so a file has the same
  name whether it was downloaded on its own or unzipped out of the account export. Japanese and
  other non-ASCII names are preserved rather than transliterated.
- Exposes a stats dashboard via pure SQL aggregation (no N+1)
- Predicts which applications have been ghosted: the dashboard flags any application sitting in
  applied or phone_screen that has been silent longer than the 90th percentile of that user's own
  historical reply times for the stage, derived from the timeline audit trail with a window
  function. Below five recorded replies at a stage it falls back to a global default (21 days for
  applied, 14 for phone_screen) and labels the threshold as a default rather than a personal one.
- Offers a Kanban board at /board: one column per active status, and dragging a card between
  columns performs a real FSM transition. The board fetches the legal moves and its column set
  from GET /api/v1/transitions rather than keeping its own copy of the table, and the server
  re-validates every move regardless. Each card also has a menu listing every legal next state,
  which is the keyboard-accessible path.
- Installs as an Android PWA and registers as a share target: share a job posting from any app
  and land in the new-application form with the AI pre-fill already reading it; a share with no
  link in it seeds a paste box instead, and the same paste box is offered when a site refuses
  the automated fetch. Install goes through Chrome — share-sheet integration lives in the
  WebAPK, which Brave cannot mint.
- Cursor-based pagination on the applications list

## Registration is closed

There is no sign-up form and no sign-up endpoint. The app holds resumes — the most PII-dense
document most people own — and it is deliberately not built to be anyone's custodian but its
author's. Visitors sign in to the shared demo account below, which is the full app with twelve
applications already in it, not a stripped sandbox. Accounts are created by the operator with
`bin/rails users:create`. Data and erasure requests go to karirkalyan@cypherpunkzero.com; there
is no self-service delete button, and the app does not claim one.

## Tech stack

- Backend: Rails 8 API-only, Ruby 3.4.9, PostgreSQL 18, Devise + devise-jwt
- Jobs & cache: Solid Queue + Solid Cache, both Postgres-backed — no Redis, no Sidekiq.
  Workers run inside Puma, so there is no separate worker service.
- Frontend: Next.js 16 App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS
- Auth: JWT issued by Rails, stored in httpOnly cookie by Next.js — never reaches client JS
- Deploy: Railway (API service + web service + managed PostgreSQL)
- Tests: RSpec (unit + request specs against real DB), Playwright E2E, and a CI script that fails
  the build when the en and ja message catalogs disagree about which keys exist

## Live

- App: https://kk.chairulakmal.com
- Architecture, and the reasoning behind it: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/about
- API overview: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/docs
- API reference (Swagger UI): https://api-production-4899.up.railway.app/api-docs
- Japanese: the same pages under /ja (e.g. https://kk.chairulakmal.com/ja/about)
- Demo account: demo@karirkalyan.com / oretachinomachida (12 pre-loaded Tokyo tech applications)
- Privacy policy: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/privacy
- Terms: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/terms
